does: This paper introduces PersonaTeaming, a novel automated red-teaming method that incorporates personas into adversarial prompt generation to discover a broader range of AI model vulnerabilities. The core contribution is a persona-based prompt mutation methodology that generates adversarial prompts from two categories: red-teaming expert personas and regular AI user personas. The work includes a dynamic persona-generating algorithm that automatically creates diverse persona types tailored to different seed prompts. By grounding adversarial generation in specific identities and backgrounds, the method explores a wider spectrum of attack strategies than conventional automated red-teaming approaches, enabling more comprehensive risk surface discovery at scale.
connects: This work extends the intersection of AI safety evaluation and automated red-teaming research. It responds to recent calls in AI governance literature emphasizing how red-teamers' identities and backgrounds fundamentally shape what risks they uncover, addressing a gap in current automated approaches that treat prompt generation as identity-agnostic. The paper builds on prior adversarial prompt generation methods and automated red-teaming frameworks by incorporating insights from human red-teaming studies showing that diverse perspectives surface different vulnerability classes. It connects to research on prompt engineering and jailbreak techniques while bridging the human-centered red-teaming tradition with scalable automation.
enables: This work opens pathways for more representative and comprehensive automated AI safety evaluation. By demonstrating how persona-informed prompt generation can expand vulnerability discovery, it establishes a foundation for incorporating demographic diversity, expertise levels, and cultural backgrounds into systematic risk assessment. The dynamic persona-generation algorithm could be extended to model broader populations of potential AI users and adversaries. This approach enables future work on identifying which persona types most effectively surface particular risk categories, personalizing red-teaming to specific deployment contexts, and developing methods that automatically discover underrepresented user perspectives that current automated systems might overlook.
